title: Cat Black Tarry Stool (Melena): Emergency Guide & Causes
meta_desc: Black, tarry stools in cats (melena) indicate digested blood from the upper GI tract. This is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate vet care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Black, tarry stool in cats is a high-priority veterinary emergency indicating internal bleeding that requires immediate medical assessment. To assist the veterinary team, take a clear photo of the stool to help them quickly prioritize your cat's care.

What does it mean if my cat has black, tarry stool?

Black, tarry, or sticky stool in cats is medically referred to as melena. This specific appearance is caused by the presence of digested blood that has originated from the upper gastrointestinal tract, such as the mouth, esophagus, stomach, or the upper part of the small intestine. Because the blood has been processed by digestive enzymes, it turns dark and takes on a thick, coal-tar consistency.

Is black, tarry stool in cats a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency level: High. Melena is considered a veterinary emergency.
  • Potential causes: Black stools often indicate significant internal bleeding from stomach ulcers, ingestion of toxins (like rodenticides), foreign body obstructions, or severe organ dysfunction.
  • When to seek immediate care: If your cat is also showing signs of lethargy, pale gums, or loss of appetite, please seek emergency veterinary services immediately.

How does taking a photo of my cat's stool help with veterinary triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the stool before cleaning it up or heading to the clinic.
  • A photo allows the triage team to quickly confirm the presence of melena and differentiate it from simply dark-colored waste.
  • This visual information helps the medical team prioritize your cat's case and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you even enter the exam room.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, black, tarry feces (melena) indicate hemorrhage in the stomach or upper part of the small intestine. While gastrointestinal ulceration is uncommonly reported in cats, neoplasia (e.g., lymphoma, adenocarcinoma) has been associated with it, though the cause is often unknown. Cats with gastrointestinal ulceration rarely show specific signs such as melena or hematemesis but frequently show signs of anorexia and underlying disease.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 156)

faqs.question: Can certain foods cause black stools in cats?
faqs.content: While some high-protein diets or specific ingredients might darken the stool, they rarely produce the sticky, tarry texture characteristic of melena. If the stool is tacky or looks like coffee grounds, it is likely blood.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of internal bleeding?
faqs.content: In addition to black stools, you may notice your cat has very pale or white gums, extreme weakness, vomiting, or a painful abdomen. These are signs of a critical situation.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if the next stool is normal?
faqs.content: No. Because melena indicates internal bleeding, waiting can lead to severe anemia or shock. It is always safer to have a veterinarian perform an assessment immediately.
